figures.caption:
Fig. 3. Barrier widths predicted by different models: PPT, TIPIS, and FDM (field-direction model), taken from [28]. At high field strengths, corresponding
to γ ≪1, the PPT curve approaches the Ip/F curve.
figures.label: Fig. 3

figures.caption:
Fig. 4. Experimentally measured electron momenta distributions after strong field ionization for different values of ellipticity, ϵ, of laser light. Peak laser
intensity: 8 × 1014 W cm−2, pulse duration (FWHM) of 33 fs, central wavelength of 788 nm.
Source: Taken from [36].
figures.label: Fig. 4

figures.caption:
Fig. 7. Larmor clock proposed by Baz’ [72] and Rybachenko [73]. Spin polarized electrons, with spin sx, enter a potential barrier where a constant magnetic
field points along the z-axis. When the electron exits the potential barrier, it acquires an additional spin component along the y-axis due to L
figures.label: Fig. 7

figures.caption:
Fig. 8. The propagator corresponds to the sum of the all possible paths between points A and B, each path, xj(t) contributing eiS[xj(t)], where S is defined
by Eq. (42).
figures.label: Fig. 8

figures.caption:
Fig. 9. Radially integrated electron momenta distributions. Tunnelling delay time is extracted from the measured angle for electrons located at the peak
of this distribution. (a) Lower intensity and clockwise polarization; (b) Higher intensity and counter-clockwise polarization.
figures.label: Fig. 9

figures.caption: Fig. 11. Tomographic reconstruction method. Reconstruction of full 3-D electron momenta distribution using projections in momentum space.
figures.label: Fig. 11

figures.caption:
Fig. 12. Top: 3D probability distribution of tunnelling times constructed using the FPI approach, taken from [22]. (a) Probability distribution at Intensity
1.6 × 1014 W/cm2. (b) Intensity 6.5 × 1014 W/cm2.
figures.label: Fig. 12

Commonly used definitions of tunnelling times. Each one depends on the transmission amplitude, T, of the
